title: Barber/ERLC Swing and Miss (Again) at Abolitionism
author: Wesley Russell
orientation: Wesley Russell and James Silberman issue a point-by-point rebuttal to SBC president Bart Barber's ERLC LIGHT Magazine article 'A Pro-Life Response to Abolitionism's Critiques,' arguing Barber misrepresents abolitionist legislation, mishandles Scripture on culpability and justice, and conflates pragmatism with biblical wisdom. The piece challenges five specific Barber claims and calls Barber to publicly meet with abolitionist pastor Dusty Deevers.
audiences: Southern Baptist pastor weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
Pro-life legislator considering an equal-protection bill
Christian wrestling with whether aborting mothers are 'second victims'
Abolitionist activist seeking biblical and legal counter-arguments
ERLC or SBC entity leader evaluating Barber's article

questions.question: How do abolition bills actually handle medically dangerous situations like ectopic pregnancy, contrary to Barber's claim that they leave women to die?
questions.audience: Pro-life legislator considering an equal-protection bill
questions.answer: Abolition bills address such situations by restating or strengthening existing legal defenses (self-defense, duress) and, in some cases, providing explicit statutory exemptions. The article cites Texas HB3326 as one example that explicitly justifies lawful medical procedures intended to remove an ectopic pregnancy when no alternative is available to save both lives. Barber's worry that doctors would be hauled into court for every such case ignores how prosecutors actually exercise discretion.
questions.quote: Conduct is justified if the conduct charged is a lawful medical procedure performed by a physician or other licensed health care provider and intended to remove an ectopic pregnancy that seriously threatens the life of the mother when a reasonable alternative to save the lives of both the mother and the unborn child is unavailable.

questions.question: Why does the authors say Barber's affirmative-defense objection is really an objection to the entire American legal system, not to abolition bills specifically?
questions.audience: Pro-life legislator considering an equal-protection bill
questions.answer: Affirmative defenses are the standard mechanism by which American criminal law (and most pro-life law) handles justified conduct. If Barber objects to doctors having to raise a defense, he is objecting to how nearly all criminal statutes work, not to anything unique to abolition bills, which simply extend existing law to children in the womb.
questions.quote: Even as a non-issue, Bart's problem is not fundamentally with abolition bills: his problem is with the entire American legal system and almost all pro-life bills that operates with affirmative defenses.

questions.question: What is wrong with Barber's classification of treatments for ectopic pregnancy as 'abortion'?
questions.audience: Christian wrestling with whether aborting mothers are 'second victims'
questions.answer: Treatments for ectopic pregnancy (salpingostomy and salpingectomy) are categorically distinct from abortion: they are not the cause of the baby's death and may save the mother's life. Calling them abortion conflates morally and medically different procedures and muddles the abolitionist position.
questions.quote: Barber, however, classifies these medical procedures as abortion, writing that these procedures "take the ectopic baby's life to save the mother."

questions.question: How does the article use James 1:14-15 to challenge the 'second victim' narrative?
questions.audience: Christian wrestling with whether aborting mothers are 'second victims'
questions.answer: James 1:14-15 teaches that each person is tempted by their own desire, which then conceives sin. External pressure from boyfriends, families, or stigma does not exonerate a person who consents to murder; Scripture locates moral responsibility in the individual who acts on sinful desire.
questions.quote: But each person is tempted when he is lured and enticed by his own desire. Then desire when it has conceived gives birth to sin, and sin when it is fully grown brings forth death.

questions.question: How do the authors expose the internal contradiction in Barber's position that aborting mothers are simultaneously victims and morally culpable?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: Barber affirms that women who consent to abortion are morally and spiritually culpable while also calling them victims in every case. The authors argue Scripture has no category for 'innocent murderers' or 'guiltless violence' and that calling someone a victim while also calling them culpable destroys the call to repentance.
questions.quote: If every woman, in every abortion, every time is a victim, then can they simultaneously be morally and spiritually culpable? There is no biblical category for what Barber is grasping for here. There is no category set aside for innocent murderers or guiltless violence.

questions.question: What is the article's clarification about whether laws can 'mandate' prosecution?
questions.audience: Pro-life legislator considering an equal-protection bill
questions.answer: No law mandates prosecution. Prosecutors retain discretion in every jurisdiction. Laws make prosecution possible; they do not bind a prosecutor to bring charges where the facts do not warrant it. The authors say Barber and the Light Magazine editors betray legal ignorance by framing it otherwise.
questions.quote: The first point which must be noted is that no law "mandates" prosecution. That's not how laws work, and this is another example of Barber's legal ignorance (and that of Light Magazine's editors) rearing its head. Laws make possible the prosecution of crimes but prosecutors have discretion regarding whether to bring charges to court.

questions.question: How does the article apply Leviticus 19 and Genesis 9:6 to argue against legal partiality toward preborn neighbors?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: Loving one's preborn neighbor as oneself, per Jesus' citation of Leviticus 19:18, requires showing no legal partiality and judging in righteousness per Leviticus 19:15. The righteous standard, per Genesis 9:6, is that whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed because man is made in God's image.
questions.quote: Meaning, if you want to love your neighbor—especially your preborn neighbor—show no legal partiality to them and judge them in righteousness. Judge them according to the righteous standard God has given us: the one that says, "Whoever sheds the blood of man, by man shall his blood be shed, for God made man in his own image" (Genesis 9:6).

questions.question: Why do the authors call Barber's appeal to pragmatism a worldview problem rather than a methodological one?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist seeking biblical and legal counter-arguments
questions.answer: Pragmatism is a distinct philosophical worldview (Peirce/James) that locates the meaning and truth of beliefs in their practical consequences. The authors argue this is categorically distinct from a Christian worldview that judges actions by God's revealed standard, not by outcomes, so labeling pragmatism as 'wisdom employed to obey God' smuggles in a non-Christian epistemology.
questions.quote: Pragmatism is not an issue of nuance—it is a philosophical worldview, one that is categorically distinct from the Christian worldview.

questions.question: How does the article respond to Barber's appeal to Mosaic case law (cities of refuge, manslaughter laws) as evidence that wisdom permits incremental application of the sixth commandment?
questions.audience: Southern Baptist pastor weighing incrementalism vs. immediatism
questions.answer: The authors say Barber conflates two different things: God justly distinguishing between categories of homicide (intentional vs. accidental) versus humans choosing to write laws that strip some preborn children of all rights and equal protection. The first is righteous categorization; the second is making the fatherless prey, per Isaiah 10:2.
questions.quote: This a conflation of God justly differentiating between categories of homicide and man choosing to write laws which deny some preborn children of all of their rights and legal equality, thereby "making the fatherless prey" (Isaiah 10:2).

questions.question: Why have abolition bills failed to pass even in pro-life states, according to the article?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activist seeking biblical and legal counter-arguments
questions.answer: Pro-life organizations themselves have organized against abolition bills. The Louisiana Abolition of Abortion Act passed committee 7-2, but after the ERLC and 75 other pro-life organizations signed an open letter against it, supporting legislators flipped and killed the bill on the House floor. The very groups complaining that abolition bills don't pass are often the reason they don't.
questions.quote: It is first and foremost pro-life organizations who have stood against bills to abolish abortion at every turn, most famously in Louisiana when the ERLC (which publishes Light Magazine and is an SBC entity) signed an open letter with 75 other pro-life organizations opposing and killing the Abolition of Abortion in Louisiana Act.

questions.question: What do the authors ask the ERLC and pro-life organizations to do at minimum if they will not support abolition bills?
questions.audience: ERLC or SBC entity leader evaluating Barber's article
questions.answer: The authors say the best outcome would be repentance and active support for abolition, but if that will not happen, the second-best outcome is for these organizations to stop actively killing abolition legislation and step aside.
questions.quote: The best outcome would be these pro-life and national SBC entities repenting and supporting the abolition of abortion, but second best would be for them to get out of the way.

questions.question: How do the authors respond to Barber's stated desire to sit across the table from abolitionists?
questions.audience: ERLC or SBC entity leader evaluating Barber's article
questions.answer: They call him to make it happen by responding to Dusty Deevers' text message. Deevers, an SBC pastor at Grace Community Church in Elgin, OK, has repeatedly sought private reconciliation per Matthew 18 and offered moderated, documented public dialogue, but Barber has refused while continuing to publicly misrepresent him.
questions.quote: As God commands of me, I have—along with the elders of my church—sought reconciliation directly with Bart from his slanders and lies and am willing and have offered multiple times to sit down across the table from Bart and have these conversations. However, at this point, that conversation would need to be moderated and documented in order stop any further distortion.

questions.question: How does the article distinguish a genuine victim of abortion from a culpable mother?
questions.audience: Christian wrestling with whether aborting mothers are 'second victims'
questions.answer: A woman who has an abortion pill slipped into her drink without her knowledge (such as the 2022 Texas case) is genuinely a victim. A mother who knowingly, mindfully, and willfully kills her child is the murderer, and the child is the only victim. Circumstances of temptation do not transfer culpability away from a willful agent.
questions.quote: The mindful, knowledgeable, and willful abortion of a child by the woman, though? That is murder and the child is the only victim.

related.title: SBC President Slanders SBC Pastor
related.reason: Directly documents the Barber/Deevers Twitter thread referenced in Claim 5 about Barber's refusal to reconcile.

related.title: Southern Baptists of Texas Testifiers Go Off the Rails While Defending Incrementalism
related.reason: Tagged with Bart Barber and treats the same SBTC/SBC defense of incrementalism this article rebuts.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Provides the foundational framing of the two ideologies the article opens with.

related.title: The Holiness of God Destroys Pro-Life Incrementalism
related.reason: Offers the deeper theological case against incrementalism that this article assumes.

related.title: Immediate, Not Gradual Abolition of Abortion
related.reason: Argues the positive case for immediatism that Barber's LIGHT Magazine piece tries to refute.

related.title: The Rise and Progress of Abortion Abolitionism in the Southern Baptist Convention
related.reason: Background on the SBC abolitionist movement and the resolution context Barber is pushing back against.

related.title: Southern Baptist Convention Moves One Step Closer to Abolitionism (And Answering Objections)
related.reason: Companion piece answering common SBC objections similar to those Barber raises.
